title: Magnetoelastic effects in the metallic frustrated antiferromagnet CrB$_2$
authors: Tadataka Watanabe, Mai Watanabe, Sakurako Suganuma, Andreas Bauer, Christian Pfleiderer
abstract: Hexagonal chromium diboride CrB$_2$ is a metallic frustrated antiferromagnet with a Néel temperature $T_N \sim$ 88 K. In CrB$_2$, Cr 3$d$ electrons not only give rise to localized magnetic moments but also contribute to metallic conduction. We perform ultrasound velocity measurements on a single crystal of hexagonal CrB$_2$ to determine its elastic properties. The temperature dependence of the $ab$-plane shear elastic modulus exhibits Curie-type softening upon cooling from $\sim$120 K down to $T_N$. This behavior is interpreted as a precursor to a symmetry-lowering lattice distortion at $T_N$, indicating that magnetic frustration is relieved via transverse magnetoelastic coupling. In addition, the $a$-axis and $c$-axis compressive elastic moduli show unusual softness and their suppression upon cooling, which are naturally explained by Fermi-surface nesting and its suppression upon cooling. The present results suggest that, in CrB$_2$, longitudinal magnetoelastic coupling suppresses Fermi-surface nesting and enhances frustrated exchange interactions, while transverse magnetoelastic coupling plays a key role in relieving the frustration.
summary: This paper uses ultrasound spectroscopy to reveal how lattice vibrations and magnetic order interact in the frustrated antiferromagnet CrB2. It demonstrates that the material uses both transverse and longitudinal magnetoelastic coupling to manage magnetic frustration and Fermi-surface instabilities. These findings provide insight into the complex magnetostructural dynamics in metallic frustrated systems.
main_problem: Investigating the interplay between lattice and magnetic degrees of freedom in the metallic frustrated antiferromagnet CrB2, specifically how magnetoelastic coupling affects magnetic frustration and Fermi-surface nesting.
main_result: The study identifies that transverse magnetoelastic coupling leads to Curie-type softening in the shear modulus C66 as a precursor to a spin-Jahn-Teller transition, while longitudinal coupling suppresses Fermi-surface nesting.
method: Ultrasound velocity measurements using the phase-comparison technique on high-quality single crystals of CrB2.
model_or_system: The physical system is CrB2, a hexagonal metallic frustrated antiferromagnet with a Cr triangular lattice and a Neel temperature of approximately 88 K.
key_observables: Elastic moduli (C11, C33, C44, C66), sound velocities (longitudinal and transverse), and lattice parameter ratio (c/a).
important_parameters: Neel temperature TN ~ 88 K, frustration ratio |thetaW/TN| ~ 8.5, and temperature range from 2 K to 300 K.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the Curie-type softening can be modeled using a mean-field expression and that conventional elastic hardening follows the Varshni equation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the CrB2 unit cell and sound propagation vectors; Figure 2 displays the temperature dependence of compressive moduli C11 and C33; Figure 3 shows the temperature dependence of shear moduli C44 and C66; Figure 5 highlights the relative variations in different moduli; Figure 6 shows the c/a ratio temperature dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material properties, describes the experimental ultrasound setup, presents the temperature dependence of various elastic moduli, analyzes the mechanisms of softening (spin-JT vs. FS nesting), and concludes with the dual role of magnetoelastic coupling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Cr sites of CrB2 in the hexagonal crystal unit cell, with the propagation vector k and polarization vector u of the sound waves for the compressive elastic moduli C11 and C33, and the shear elastic moduli C44 and C66. The sound velocities of the longitudinal (vL) and transverse (vT ) waves corresponding to these elastic moduli of CrB2 measured at a temperature of 300 K are listed on the right.

FIG. 4: C66(T ) of CrB2 in the range 80 K ≤T ≤130 K [markers, from Fig. 3(b)]. The labeled arrows indicate TN ∼ 88 K for CrB2. The solid curve is a ﬁt of Eq. (1) to the experimental C66(T ) in the range TN ≤T ≤120 K; the values for the ﬁt parameters are also listed. The dashed horizontal line indicates C0 66 in Eq. (1).

FIG. 5: Comparison of the relative shifts of the compressive moduli C11(T ) and C33(T ), and the shear moduli C44(T ) and C66(T ) of CrB2 [markers, from Figs. 2 and 3]. The plotted curves have been vertically shifted for clarity. The solid dou- ble arrows at the right side of the ﬁgure indicate the variation in the experimental CΓ(T ), which is deﬁned as the diﬀerence between the maximum and the minimum values of CΓ(T ). The minimum occurs at the highest measured T = 300 K for all the elastic moduli, and the maximum occurs at the lowest measured T = 2 K for C11(T ), C33(T ), and C44(T ), and at T ∼120 K for C66(T ). The inset is a schematic of CΓ(T ) typically observed for solids,...

FIG. 6: (a) Ratios of hexagonal lattice constants, c/a, for the antiferromagnet CrB2 and paramagnets (Cr0.3Mo0.7)B2 and VB2 as functions of T [from Ref. [14]]. (b) The moduli C11(T ) and (c) C33(T ) of CrB2 [markers, from Figs. 2(a) and 2(b), respectively]. The dashed curves in (b) and (c) are ﬁts of Eq. (2) to the experimental CΓ(T ) in the range 2 K ≤T ≤ 50 K; the values for the ﬁt parameters are also listed. (d) Schematics of low-T and high-T longitudinal acoustic phonon branches in CrB2, where the high-T branch is softened due to FS nesting as indicated by the arrow.
